source_paper_title: Square-Root Regret Bounds for Continuous-Time Episodic Markov Decision Processes

context:
Page 14, Section 4 (Main Results), discussion immediately after Theorem 2.

While the bounds on \(K\) are the most important as they inform the convergence rates of learning algorithms, it remains a significant open question whether one can improve the dependence of these bounds on \(S\), \(H\) and \(\lambda_{\max}\) to narrow down the gap between the upper and lower bounds.
title: Improve minimax regret dependence on states and horizon in episodic CTMDPs
background_latex:
Consider an episodic, finite-horizon continuous-time Markov decision process (CTMDP) with finite state space \(\mathcal S\) (\(|\mathcal S|=S\)), finite action space \(\mathcal A\) (\(|\mathcal A|=A\)), and fixed horizon \([0,H]\). When action \(a\in\mathcal A\) is chosen in state \(x\in\mathcal S\), the process stays in \(x\) for an exponentially distributed holding time with rate \(\lambda(x,a)\in[\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max}]\), accrues reward at rate \(r(x,a)\in[0,1]\) during the holding time, and then jumps to a next state \(Y\in\mathcal S\) with probability \(p(\cdot\mid x,a)\) (time-homogeneous). The parameters \(p\) and \(\lambda\) are unknown to the learning agent.

A (deterministic Markov) policy \(\pi\) maps a state and remaining time to an action: \(\pi:\mathcal S\times[0,H]\to\mathcal A\). Starting from a fixed initial state \(x_0\), define the (finite-horizon) value function
\[
V^{\pi}(x,t):=\mathbb E_{\pi}\Big[\int_0^{t} r(X(u),A(u))\,du\,\Big|\,X(0)=x\Big],\qquad (x,t)\in\mathcal S\times[0,H],
\]
and the optimal value \(V^*(x,t):=\sup_{\pi} V^{\pi}(x,t)\).

The agent interacts with the same unknown CTMDP over \(K\) independent episodes, each of length \(H\), restarting from \(x_0\) each episode. Let \(\pi_k\) be the policy used in episode \(k\). The (expected) cumulative regret after \(K\) episodes is
\[
\mathrm{Regret}(K):=\mathbb E\Big[\sum_{k=1}^K \big(V^*(x_0,H)-V^{\pi_k}(x_0,H)\big)\Big].
\]
A minimax (worst-case) regret rate studies \(\sup_{M\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{Regret}(K)\), where \(\mathcal C\) is the class of all such CTMDPs with given \((S,A,H,\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max})\) and bounded rewards.

In the referenced work, an upper bound of order \(\widetilde O(\sqrt K)\) is proved but with a leading dependence that is worse than the known lower bound in its dependence on \(S\), on \(H\), and on \(\lambda_{\max}\). In particular, their regret upper bound contains an extra factor \(\sqrt S\) (relative to discrete-time tight bounds) and a worse dependence on \(H\), while their lower bound scales on the order of \(H\sqrt{SAK}\) (up to constants and log factors) in a certain parameter regime.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether one can design an episodic reinforcement-learning algorithm for the above finite-horizon tabular CTMDP class \(\mathcal C\) whose worst-case expected regret \(\sup_{M\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{Regret}(K)\) improves the known upper bounds by reducing their dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\), thereby narrowing the gap to the known lower bounds (which already show \(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) scaling in \(K\) and \(A\) up to log factors).

Equivalently: improve the minimax, instance-independent regret upper bound for episodic finite-horizon tabular CTMDPs beyond the existing \(\widetilde O(\sqrt K)\) bound by achieving sharper polynomial dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\) (and ideally matching the lower bound up to logarithmic factors).
latex:

Determine whether one can design an episodic reinforcement-learning algorithm for the above finite-horizon tabular CTMDP class \(\mathcal C\) whose worst-case expected regret \(\sup_{M\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{Regret}(K)\) improves the known upper bounds by reducing their dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\), thereby narrowing the gap to the known lower bounds (which already show \(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) scaling in \(K\) and \(A\) up to log factors).

location_in_paper: Page 14, Section 4 (Main Results), discussion immediately after Theorem 2.
open_problem_quote: While the bounds on \(K\) are the most important as they inform the convergence rates of learning algorithms, it remains a significant open question whether one can improve the dependence of these bounds on \(S\), \(H\) and \(\lambda_{\max}\) to narrow down the gap between the upper and lower bounds.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is a broad algorithmic upper-bound improvement question rather than a crisp universally quantified conjecture that can be falsified by a single finite instance. Automated search could explore small CTMDPs to guess scaling, but it cannot directly refute or confirm existence of improved algorithms.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate exploration bonuses/planning surrogates and score them by empirical regret on simulated CTMDP families, providing a computable fitness signal. However, empirical performance is only a proxy for minimax regret guarantees, so automated search may suggest promising algorithmic designs but cannot certify the improved theoretical dependence without additional proof work.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Gao & Zhou, 2023) establishes the first minimax-style \(\widetilde O(\sqrt K)\) regret guarantee for episodic finite-horizon tabular CTMDPs with unknown transition probabilities and holding-time rates, via CT-UCBVI: optimism over confidence sets combined with a continuous-time value-iteration operator \(G\) (built from \(T^a\) in (6)) and a contraction-based control of planning error. Their analysis highlights continuous-time-specific obstacles absent in discrete time: truncated holding times at the horizon, random and unbounded numbers of jumps per episode (handled via uniformization), and the need to estimate \(\lambda(x,a)\) from time-spent statistics rather than counts.

However, the resulting leading dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\) is not tight: the upper bound carries an extra \(\sqrt S\) factor (stemming from \(\ell_1\) concentration for the \(S\)-dimensional transition vector) and worse polynomial dependence on \(H\) and \(\lambda_{\max}\) than the lower bound. The paper’s lower bound construction (tree MDP with Erlang-distributed time-to-depth \(d\)) yields \(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) in a regime, matching the \(\sqrt K\) and \(\sqrt{SA}\) scaling but leaving a substantial gap in \(S\) and \(H\).

The only forward-citing work provided here does not close this gap. Gao & Zhou’s later work on average-reward CTMDPs develops refined continuous-time UCRL-style tools (confidence bounds for exponential holding times, uniformization, and point-process comparisons) but targets instance-dependent \(O(\log T)\) regret rather than episodic minimax rates. A separate intensity-control actor–critic paper develops martingale-based continuous-time policy-gradient machinery without regret bounds. Thus, based on the available forward citations, improving minimax episodic CTMDP regret dependence on \(S,H,\lambda_{\max}\) remains open; promising directions suggested by the source include importing variance-sensitive (Bernstein) bonuses or value-function-based confidence sets (as in UCBVI-BF/EULER) while coping with random jump counts and horizon truncation.

sources.title: Logarithmic regret bounds for continuous-time average-reward Markov decision processes
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies tabular continuous-time MDPs in the infinite-horizon average-reward setting with unknown transition probabilities \(p(\cdot\mid s,a)\) and holding-time rates \(\lambda(s,a)\in[\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max}]\). It proves an instance-dependent logarithmic regret lower bound: for any uniformly fast algorithm \(\mathcal G\) and any CTMDP \(M\), \[\liminf_{T\to\infty}\frac{\mathbb E[R_T^{\mathcal G}(s_0,M)]}{\log T}\ge C(M),\] where \(C(M)=\sum_{(s,a)\in B(M)} \phi^*(s,a;M)/K(s,a;M)\) is defined via suboptimality gaps \(\phi^*\) and KL-type information terms \(K\). It also proposes CT-UCRL and proves a finite-time gap-dependent upper bound (in decision epochs \(N\)): \[R_N^{\mathrm{CT\text{-}UCRL}}(s_0,M)\le \frac{4C\,\log N}{g}+C',\] where \(g\) is the average-reward policy gap and \(C\) scales polynomially in \(S,A,\lambda_{\max},\lambda_{\min}^{-1}\) and the diameter \(D(M)\) (e.g. \(C=\Theta(\lambda_{\max}^2 D(M)^2 S^2A + (\lambda_{\max}^2/\lambda_{\min}^2)SA)\) up to constants).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns minimax (instance-independent) regret for finite-horizon episodic CTMDPs and improving its dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\) toward \(\widetilde\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\). This paper does not address the episodic finite-horizon setting or minimax \(\widetilde O(\sqrt K)\) bounds; instead it studies infinite-horizon average-reward CTMDPs and derives instance-dependent \(O(\log T)\) regret (gap-dependent) plus matching logarithmic lower bounds for uniformly fast algorithms. Nevertheless, it contributes techniques potentially relevant to episodic CTMDPs: (i) careful estimation/confidence bounds for exponential holding times (truncated mean with sub-Gaussian tails), (ii) continuous-time UCRL-style optimism with an extended CTMDP and uniformization, and (iii) stochastic comparison of counting processes to relate decision-epoch and continuous-time regret. These tools might be adaptable when trying to sharpen \(S,H,\lambda_{\max}\) dependence in episodic minimax analyses, but the paper itself does not narrow the stated minimax gap.

sources.title: Reinforcement learning for intensity control: An application to choice-based network revenue management
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops a continuous-time, event-driven actor–critic reinforcement-learning framework for intensity control problems (e.g., choice-based network revenue management) by adapting Monte Carlo / TD policy evaluation and policy-gradient methods to continuous time without upfront time discretization. Key theoretical statements include: (i) a martingale characterization of the policy value function (Proposition 1) and an equivalent martingale orthogonality condition (Theorem 2) for policy evaluation; (ii) an implementable continuous-time policy-gradient formula for jump/intensity-control dynamics (Theorem 3): \[

abla_\phi J(0,c;\pi_\phi)=\mathbb E\Big[\int_{(0,T]}\sum_{j=1}^n 
abla_\phi\log\pi_\phi(S_t\mid t,X_{t-}) ig(J(t,X_{t-}-A_j;\pi_\phi)-J(t,X_{t-};\pi_\phi)+p_j ig)\,dN_{j,t}^{\pi_\phi}+\gamma\int_0^T 
abla_\phi H(\pi_\phi(\cdot\mid t,X_{t-}))\,dt\Big].
\]
sources.connection: The open problem asks for improved minimax regret upper bounds (tight dependence on \(S,H,\lambda_{\max}\)) for episodic finite-horizon tabular CTMDPs with unknown transition kernel and holding rates. This paper does not study regret, minimax rates, or tabular exploration; it focuses on model-free actor–critic methods for a specific class of event-driven intensity control problems (Poisson arrivals, revenue-management structure), provides martingale-based foundations for policy evaluation/gradient, and reports empirical performance. While its event-driven ‘inherent discretization’ idea and martingale tools may be conceptually relevant to CTMDP algorithm design, it does not provide regret guarantees nor address the stated gap in \(S,H,\lambda_{\max}\) dependence.
